tool_calls.name: submit_final_result
tool_calls.args:
{"final_result": "
## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters for Segment Comparisons

### Definition

The SpaceX S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a segment's GAAP **income (loss) from operations**, adjusted by adding back four specific line items. It is the filing's primary non-GAAP segment performance measure, used by management for internal planning, forecasting, and for investors to evaluate operating performance across periods.

---

### The Four Add-Backs (from Segment Operating Income)

| # | Add-Back | Nature |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Depreciation and amortization** | Non-cash; allocation of tangible and intangible asset costs over useful lives |
| 2 | **Share-based compensation (SBC)** | Non-cash; expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees |
| 3 | **Restructuring charges** | Typically non-recurring; severance, facility closures, organizational reorganizations |
| 4 | **Impairment** | Non-cash write-down of long-lived assets when recoverable value falls below carrying amount |

The formula is:

> **Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Segment Income (Loss) from Operations + D&A + SBC + Restructuring + Impairment**

---

### Illustrative Reconciliation — FY 2025 (in millions)

| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Depreciation & amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

The same GAAP operating loss of $(2,589) million is transformed into an Adjusted EBITDA of $6,584 million — a swing of $9,173 million, driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($6,701M) and SBC ($1,947M).

---

### Why Understanding Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

**1. The add-backs can reverse the profitability ranking of segments.**
In FY 2025, the **AI segment** had a GAAP operating loss of $(6,355)M but a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of only $(1,237)M — a $5,118M improvement driven by $3,568M of D&A and $1,063M of SBC add-backs. Without understanding this, an analyst would overstate AI's operational viability relative to its actual cash burn trajectory.

**2. Share-based compensation distorts inter-segment comparability.**
SBC is allocated unevenly across segments. In Q1 2026, the AI segment's SBC add-back was $378M versus $145M for Space and $116M for Connectivity. Because SBC represents real economic cost to shareholders (dilution), removing it inflates the apparent profitability of whichever segment grants more equity compensation. A segment with heavy SBC will appear to have better Adjusted EBITDA margins than one paying in cash, even if both have identical economic cost structures.

**3. Depreciation & amortization masks asset intensity differences.**
D&A was the largest single add-back in every period (e.g., $6,701M in FY 2025). The AI segment alone had $3,568M of D&A, reflecting massive capital expenditures on compute infrastructure. Removing D&A makes AI and Connectivity look more comparable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis, but the underlying asset base and required reinvestment differ enormously. Ignoring D&A can lead to overstating free cash flow generation capacity for capital-intensive segments.

**4. Restructuring and impairment charges are lumpy and non-comparable across periods.**
The AI segment recorded $487M of restructuring charges in FY 2025 (essentially all corporate restructuring), while Space had $38M of impairment. In Q1 2026, the AI segment actually recorded a *negative* restructuring add-back of $(11)M (a reversal). These items create period-over-period volatility that has nothing to do with recurring operational performance. Excluding them smooths earnings but can obscure real costs of strategic missteps or asset impairments.

**5. The filing's own cautionary language underscores non-comparability.**
The S-1 explicitly warns that "other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures." This means cross-company peer benchmarking using Adjusted EBITDA is inherently unreliable without normalizing for differing add-back definitions.

**6. Common-control accounting compounds the issue.**
The filing's financial statements have been retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings for all periods. This means the SBC, D&A, restructuring, and impairment figures for each segment reflect the consolidated post-merger entity, not standalone SpaceX. Historical trends in the add-backs may reflect consolidation effects rather than organic changes in segment economics.

---

### Summary

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful management tool for viewing cash-generative capacity before capital allocation decisions, but it is constructed by stripping out **$9.2 billion** of real costs in FY 2025 alone. Before comparing segment-to-segment economics — or comparing SpaceX's segments to peers — an analyst must understand:

- **Which add-backs dominate** (D&A is ~73% of total add-backs in FY 2025)
- **How unevenly they are distributed** across segments
- **Whether they represent truly non-recurring items** (restructuring/impairment) or **recurring economic costs** (SBC, D&A)
- **That the filing itself disclaims cross-company comparability** of this measure

Without this decomposition, Segment Adjusted EBITDA can materially misrepresent the relative profitability and capital needs of each business line.

---

### Sources

| Source | Name |
|--------|------|
| https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm | SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) — MD&A Non-GAAP Reconciliation Tables and Segment Footnote |
"}

## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters for Segment Comparisons

### Definition

The SpaceX S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a segment's GAAP **income (loss) from operations**, adjusted by adding back four specific line items. It is the filing's primary non-GAAP segment performance measure, used by management for internal planning, forecasting, and for investors to evaluate operating performance across periods.

---

### The Four Add-Backs (from Segment Operating Income)

| # | Add-Back | Nature |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Depreciation and amortization** | Non-cash; allocation of tangible and intangible asset costs over useful lives |
| 2 | **Share-based compensation (SBC)** | Non-cash; expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees |
| 3 | **Restructuring charges** | Typically non-recurring; severance, facility closures, organizational reorganizations |
| 4 | **Impairment** | Non-cash write-down of long-lived assets when recoverable value falls below carrying amount |

The formula is:

> **Segment Adjusted EBITDA = Segment Income (Loss) from Operations + D&A + SBC + Restructuring + Impairment**

---

### Illustrative Reconciliation — FY 2025 (in millions)

| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Depreciation & amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

The same GAAP operating loss of $(2,589) million is transformed into an Adjusted EBITDA of $6,584 million — a swing of $9,173 million, driven overwhelmingly by D&A ($6,701M) and SBC ($1,947M).

---

### Why Understanding Construction Matters Before Comparing Segment Economics

**1. The add-backs can reverse the profitability ranking of segments.**
In FY 2025, the **AI segment** had a GAAP operating loss of $(6,355)M but a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of only $(1,237)M — a $5,118M improvement driven by $3,568M of D&A and $1,063M of SBC add-backs. Without understanding this, an analyst would overstate AI's operational viability relative to its actual cash burn trajectory.

**2. Share-based compensation distorts inter-segment comparability.**
SBC is allocated unevenly across segments. In Q1 2026, the AI segment's SBC add-back was $378M versus $145M for Space and $116M for Connectivity. Because SBC represents real economic cost to shareholders (dilution), removing it inflates the apparent profitability of whichever segment grants more equity compensation. A segment with heavy SBC will appear to have better Adjusted EBITDA margins than one paying in cash, even if both have identical economic cost structures.

**3. Depreciation & amortization masks asset intensity differences.**
D&A was the largest single add-back in every period (e.g., $6,701M in FY 2025). The AI segment alone had $3,568M of D&A, reflecting massive capital expenditures on compute infrastructure. Removing D&A makes AI and Connectivity look more comparable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis, but the underlying asset base and required reinvestment differ enormously. Ignoring D&A can lead to overstating free cash flow generation capacity for capital-intensive segments.

**4. Restructuring and impairment charges are lumpy and non-comparable across periods.**
The AI segment recorded $487M of restructuring charges in FY 2025 (essentially all corporate restructuring), while Space had $38M of impairment. In Q1 2026, the AI segment actually recorded a *negative* restructuring add-back of $(11)M (a reversal). These items create period-over-period volatility that has nothing to do with recurring operational performance. Excluding them smooths earnings but can obscure real costs of strategic missteps or asset impairments.

**5. The filing's own cautionary language underscores non-comparability.**
The S-1 explicitly warns that "other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures." This means cross-company peer benchmarking using Adjusted EBITDA is inherently unreliable without normalizing for differing add-back definitions.

**6. Common-control accounting compounds the issue.**
The filing's financial statements have been retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings for all periods. This means the SBC, D&A, restructuring, and impairment figures for each segment reflect the consolidated post-merger entity, not standalone SpaceX. Historical trends in the add-backs may reflect consolidation effects rather than organic changes in segment economics.

---

### Summary

Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a useful management tool for viewing cash-generative capacity before capital allocation decisions, but it is constructed by stripping out **$9.2 billion** of real costs in FY 2025 alone. Before comparing segment-to-segment economics — or comparing SpaceX's segments to peers — an analyst must understand:

- **Which add-backs dominate** (D&A is ~73% of total add-backs in FY 2025)
- **How unevenly they are distributed** across segments
- **Whether they represent truly non-recurring items** (restructuring/impairment) or **recurring economic costs** (SBC, D&A)
- **That the filing itself disclaims cross-company comparability** of this measure

Without this decomposition, Segment Adjusted EBITDA can materially misrepresent the relative profitability and capital needs of each business line.

---

### Sources

| Source | Name |
|--------|------|
| https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm | SpaceX S-1 Filing (May 20, 2026) — MD&A Non-GAAP Reconciliation Tables and Segment Footnote |

## Segment Adjusted EBITDA: Construction and Why It Matters for Segment Comparisons

### Definition

The SpaceX S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** as a segment's GAAP **income (loss) from operations**, adjusted by adding back four specific line items. It is the filing's primary non-GAAP segment performance measure, used by management for internal planning, forecasting, and for investors to evaluate operating performance across periods.

| # | Add-Back | Nature |
|---|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Depreciation and amortization** | Non-cash; allocation of tangible and intangible asset costs over useful lives |
| 2 | **Share-based compensation (SBC)** | Non-cash; expense related to stock-based awards granted to employees |
| 3 | **Restructuring charges** | Typically non-recurring; severance, facility closures, organizational reorganizations |
| 4 | **Impairment** | Non-cash write-down of long-lived assets when recoverable value falls below carrying amount |

The formula is:

---

| | Space | Connectivity | AI | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Income (loss) from operations** | $(657) | $4,423 | $(6,355) | $(2,589) |
| Depreciation & amortization | 757 | 2,376 | 3,568 | 6,701 |
| Share-based compensation | 515 | 369 | 1,063 | 1,947 |
| Restructuring charges | — | — | 487 | 487 |
| Impairment | 38 | — | — | 38 |
| **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** | **$653** | **$7,168** | **$(1,237)** | **$6,584** |

**1. The add-backs can reverse the profitability ranking of segments.**
In FY 2025, the **AI segment** had a GAAP operating loss of $(6,355)M but a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of only $(1,237)M — a $5,118M improvement driven by $3,568M of D&A and $1,063M of SBC add-backs. **2. Share-based compensation distorts inter-segment comparability.**
SBC is allocated unevenly across segments. In Q1 2026, the AI segment's SBC add-back was $378M versus $145M for Space and $116M for Connectivity. Because SBC represents real economic cost to shareholders (dilution), removing it inflates the apparent profitability of whichever segment grants more equity compensation. A segment with heavy SBC will appear to have better Adjusted EBITDA margins than one paying in cash, even if both have identical economic cost structures.

**3. Depreciation & amortization masks asset intensity differences.**
D&A was the largest single add-back in every period (e.g., $6,701M in FY 2025). The AI segment alone had $3,568M of D&A, reflecting massive capital expenditures on compute infrastructure. Removing D&A makes AI and Connectivity look more comparable on an Adjusted EBITDA basis, but the underlying asset base and required reinvestment differ enormously. Ignoring D&A can lead to overstating free cash flow generation capacity for capital-intensive segments.

**4. Restructuring and impairment charges are lumpy and non-comparable across periods.**
The AI segment recorded $487M of restructuring charges in FY 2025 (essentially all corporate restructuring), while Space had $38M of impairment. In Q1 2026, the AI segment actually recorded a *negative* restructuring add-back of $(11)M (a reversal). These items create period-over-period volatility that has nothing to do with recurring operational performance. Excluding them smooths earnings but can obscure real costs of strategic missteps or asset impairments.

**5. The filing's own cautionary language underscores non-comparability.**
The S-1 explicitly warns that "other companies in our industry may calculate Adjusted EBITDA and Segment Adjusted EBITDA differently than we do or may not calculate them at all, limiting their usefulness as comparative measures." The filing's financial statements have been retrospectively recast to include xAI and X Holdings for all periods. This means the SBC, D&A, restructuring, and impairment figures for each segment reflect the consolidated post-merger entity, not standalone SpaceX.
